perturbation.original_text: The Contractor shall also furnish statements, reports and information to the Paying Agent to the extent that the Company is required to furnish or cause to be furnished such statements, reports or information to the Paying Agent under the Paying Agent Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The Contractor may furnish statements, reports and information to the Paying Agent.
perturbation.explanation: This modification removes the mandatory nature of furnishing required statements, reports, and information to the Paying Agent. This may violate Minnesota Statutes relating to securities and required disclosures.
perturbation.law_explanation: MN ST §80A.62 establishes civil liability for violations of the statute. By removing the mandatory requirement to furnish statements, reports, and information to the paying agent (information which would be required to be disclosed under securities regulations), the modified text creates a potential violation of securities laws, increasing civil liability risk for the company and potentially harming investors if information is withheld.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Sec. 80A.62 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes SECURITIES Chapter 80A Section 80A.62 80A.61 80A.63 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 80A Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 80A.62 Version List Recent History 2006 80A.62 New 2006 c 196 art 1 s 22 80A.62 SECTION 407; SUCCESSION AND CHANGE IN REGISTRATION OF BROKER-DEALER OR INVESTMENT ADVISER. (a) Succession. A broker-dealer or investment adviser may succeed to the current registration of another broker-dealer or investment adviser or a notice filing of a federal covered investment adviser, and a federal covered investment adviser may succeed to the current registration of an investment adviser or notice filing of another federal covered investment adviser, by filing as a successor an application for registration pursuant to section 80A.56 or 80A.58 or a notice pursuant to section 80A.60 for the unexpired portion of the current registration or notice filing. (b) Organizational change. A broker-dealer or investment adviser that changes its form of organization or state of incorporation or organization may continue its registration by filing an amendment to its registration if the change does not involve a material change in its financial condition or management. The amendment becomes effective when filed or on a date designated by the registrant in its filing. The new organization is a successor to the original registrant for the purposes of this chapter. If there is a material change in financial condition or management, the broker-dealer or investment adviser shall file a new application for registration. A predecessor registered under this chapter shall sto
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates that the Contractor furnish statements, reports, and information to the Paying Agent as required by the Company under the Paying Agent Agreement. The changed text makes this optional, stating the Contractor 'may' furnish these items. The Law Explanation states that MN ST §80A.62 establishes civil liability for violations of the statute and that the removal of the mandatory requirement creates a potential violation of securities laws, increasing civil liability risk for the company and potentially harming investors. Snippet 2 shows the title of this statute; 'Sec. 80A.62 MN Statutes'. Since the original text ensured compliance, and the change now makes this optional, it creates a legal contradiction because it introduces the possibility of failing to comply with securities laws.

perturbation.original_text: The Contractor shall exercise due care in handling and delivering the original documents and the other documents in the Note files and records. The Contractor shall not grant or allow any person an interest in original documents or rights thereunder, and all original documents in the possession of the Contractor shall be deemed to be in the possession of the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: The Contractor shall take reasonable steps in handling the documents in the Note files and records. The Contractor may grant or allow any person an interest in original documents or rights thereunder.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'due care' and replacing it with 'reasonable steps' reduces the standard of care expected of the contractor. Permitting the contractor to grant an interest in original documents introduces potential conflicts of interest and lack of transparency, potentially running afoul of Minnesota statutes concerning fiduciary duties and securities fraud. In short, this impacts the safekeeping requirements of client information under Minnesota Statute.
perturbation.law_explanation: Minnesota Statute 60A.031 imposes fiduciary responsibilities on those handling client funds, including safeguarding client assets. By reducing the standard of care and allowing the Contractor to grant interests in original documents, the altered language increases the risk of misappropriation or loss of investor assets, contradicting the fiduciary duty mandated by the statute. Client information is not being properly safeguarded as a result.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Sec. 60A.031 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes INSURANCE Chapter 60A Section 60A.031 60A.03 60A.032 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 60A Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 60A.031 Version List Recent History 2022 Subd. 6 Amended 2022 c 93 art 2 s 18 2022 Subd. 10 New 2022 c 93 art 2 s 19 2022 Subd. 11 New 2022 c 93 art 2 s 20 2020 Subd. 4 Amended 2020 c 80 art 1 s 14 2017 Subd. 4 Amended 2017 c 39 s 1 2017 Subd. 6 Amended 2017 c 39 s 2 2010 Subd. 4 Amended 2010 c 384 s 4 2004 Subd. 4 Amended 2004 c 290 s 22 2004 Subd. 4 Amended 2004 c 285 art 4 s 3 60A.031 EXAMINATIONS. § Subdivision 1. Power to examine. (1) Insurers and other licensees. At any time and for any reason related to the enforcement of the insurance laws, or to ensure that companies are being operated in a safe and sound manner and to protect the public interest, the commissioner may examine the affairs and conditions of any foreign or domestic insurance or reinsurance company, including reciprocals and fraternals, licensee or applicant for a license under the insurance laws, or any other person or organization of persons doing or in the process of organizing to do any insurance business in this state, and of any licensed advisory organization serving any of the foregoing in this state. The commissioner shall examine the affairs and conditions of every insurer licensed in this state not less frequently than once every five years. (2) Who may be examined. The commissioner in making any examination of an insurance company as authorized by this section may, if in the commissioner's discretion, there is cause to believe the commissioner is unable
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates 'due care' in handling documents and prohibits granting interests in original documents. The changed text weakens the standard of care to 'reasonable steps' and permits granting interests. The Law Explanation states that Minnesota Statute 60A.031 imposes fiduciary responsibilities, including safeguarding client assets. The altered language increasing the risk of misappropriation contradicts this fiduciary duty. Snippet 2 shows the full statute is '60A.031 EXAMINATIONS' which supports the view that the contractor needs to be examined and thus held to a standard of safeguarding client assets, but is not being properly safeguarded as a result.

perturbation.original_text: All costs of conversion and transfer of such records to the Company or another agent shall be paid by the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: All costs of conversion and transfer of such records to the Company or another agent may be paid by the Company.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'shall be paid' to 'may be paid', the contract introduces uncertainty about who will bear the costs of transferring records upon termination. This could violate Minnesota Statute, particularly rules about keeping accurate books and records.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Minnesota Statutes §302A.461 Books and Records; Inspection.
perturbation.law_explanation: Minnesota Statute 302A.461 mandates the keeping of correct and complete books and records, and that shareholders can inspect such documents. If the records are not transferred correctly, this may pose issues with shareholders being able to view required documents under Minnesota Law. It further places the company in a position to be unable to fully comply with the law.

More info... 302A.461 subd. 4 has been amended by Chapter 11, Section 18 Note: see session law sections for effective dates. 302A.461 BOOKS AND RECORDS; INSPECTION. § Subdivision 1. Share register; dates of issuance. (a) A corporation shall keep at its principal executive office, or at another place or places within the United States determined by the board, a share register not more than one year old, containing the names and addresses of the shareholders and the number and classes of shares held by each shareholder. (b) A corporation shall also keep, at its principal executive office, or at another place or places within the United States determined by the board, a record of the dates on which certificated or uncertificated shares were issued. § Subd. 2. Other documents required. A corporation shall keep at its principal executive office or at another place or places within the United States determined by the board, and, if its principal executive office or any such other place is outside of this state, shall make available at its registered office or its principal executive office within this state within ten days aft
perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall be paid' to 'may be paid' weakens the obligation regarding the costs of record transfer. While Minnesota Statute 302A.461, as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 2, focuses on maintaining and inspecting books and records, the Law Explanation suggests that improper record transfer could hinder compliance with this statute. The original contract ensures the company covers transfer costs, but the modified version creates uncertainty, potentially allowing the company to avoid these costs. This uncertainty increases the risk of non-compliance with keeping complete and accurate records, thus creating a contradiction.
